# arpdvark

> ARP network scanner for Linux. Discovers devices on the local subnet, resolves hostnames, identifies hardware vendors, and supports persistent device labels.

## Quick start

Requires Linux and root/CAP_NET_RAW. Install from source:

    git clone https://github.com/fabioconcina/arpdvark
    cd arpdvark && make build
    sudo ./arpdvark

## CLI modes

### Interactive TUI (default)

    sudo arpdvark [-i <interface>] [-t <seconds>] [--large]

Full-screen terminal UI with auto-refresh, keyboard navigation, label editing, and device detail view.

Key bindings: q/ctrl+c quit; r rescan; up/down navigate; e edit label; left/right cycle sort column; s toggle sort direction; / filter (/ again to clear); o toggle offline; Enter open detail view.
Detail view: shows all fields untruncated (IP, MAC, hostname, label, vendor, status, first/last seen) plus a weekly activity heatmap; up/down to navigate.
Sorting: left/right arrows cycle through IP, MAC, Hostname, Label, Vendor, Last Seen; s toggles asc/desc.
Filtering: / opens filter input; matches against all fields; Enter applies, Esc clears filter, / again also clears active filter.
New device alerts: devices not in state.json from previous runs are highlighted green with a count in the status bar.

### JSON output (--json)

    sudo arpdvark --json [-i <interface>] [--large]

Single scan, JSON array to stdout. Suitable for piping to jq, scripts, or other tools.

Output format:

    [
      {
        "ip": "192.168.1.1",
        "mac": "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff",
        "vendor": "Cisco Systems",
        "hostname": "router.local",
        "label": "main-router",
        "first_seen": "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z",
        "last_seen": "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"
      }
    ]

With --all, includes an "online" field (true/false) for each device.

### Human-readable table (--once)

    sudo arpdvark --once [-i <interface>] [--large]

Single scan, tab-aligned text table to stdout. Parseable with awk/cut.

### MCP server (--mcp)

    arpdvark --mcp

Runs an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server on stdio. Exposes a `scan_network` tool.

Tool: scan_network
  Parameters:
    - interface (string, optional): network interface name (auto-detected if omitted)
    - allow_large (boolean, optional): allow subnets larger than /16 (default: false)
  Returns: JSON array of device objects (same schema as --json output)

Claude Desktop configuration example:

    {
      "mcpServers": {
        "arpdvark": {
          "command": "sudo",
          "args": ["/path/to/arpdvark", "--mcp"]
        }
      }
    }

## Exit codes

- 0: success (devices found)
- 1: error (permissions, interface not found, scan failure)
- 2: scan completed but no devices found (--json and --once only)

## Flags

    -i <interface>  Network interface (default: auto-detect)
    -t <seconds>    Refresh interval for TUI mode (default: 10)
    --large         Allow subnets larger than /16
    --json          Single scan, JSON output, exit
    --once          Single scan, text table output, exit
    --all           Include offline devices (--json and --once only)
    --mcp           Run as MCP server (stdio)
    --version       Print version and exit

## Subcommands

    arpdvark forget [MAC ...]           Remove specific devices from state
    arpdvark forget --older-than N      Remove devices not seen in N days

## Permissions

## Scanning behavior

The first ARP sweep sends 3 rounds of requests (100 ms apart) to catch slow responders
such as Wi-Fi clients in power-save mode. Subsequent scans send a single round since
the device table accumulates across sweeps.

## Permissions

Uses raw ARP sockets (AF_PACKET). Requires sudo or CAP_NET_RAW:

    sudo setcap cap_net_raw+ep ./arpdvark

## Labels

Devices can be labeled by MAC address. Labels persist in ~/.config/arpdvark/tags.json
and are included in all output modes (TUI, JSON, table, MCP).

## Device persistence

Scan results are saved to ~/.config/arpdvark/state.json across runs. Each device is
keyed by MAC address and tracks IP, vendor, hostname, first_seen, and last_seen.

## Activity tracking

Every scan mode (TUI, --json, --once) records device sightings in a per-MAC 7x24 matrix
(weekday x hour) stored in ~/.config/arpdvark/activity.json. Each scan increments the
count for the current weekday+hour for every online device. The detail view renders this
as an ASCII heatmap using block characters (space, light, medium, dark, full) normalized
to the maximum count. For continuous data collection, run periodic scans via cron:
Add to root's crontab (sudo crontab -e):
*/5 * * * * SUDO_USER=youruser /path/to/arpdvark --once > /dev/null 2>&1
SUDO_USER ensures data is stored in the invoking user's home, not root's.
The `forget` subcommand also removes activity data for forgotten devices.

In the TUI, previously known devices appear immediately on startup (dimmed as offline)
before the first scan completes. Press `o` to toggle showing offline devices.

With --json --all or --once --all, offline devices are included in the output with an
"online" field (true/false for JSON, STATUS column for text table).

Use `arpdvark forget` to remove devices from the state file.
